planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil with a trellis for support in zone 9–12; White Bitter Melon is a pale-cream to white-skinned variety of the bitter melon vine (Momordica charantia var. alba) — widely grown in South and Southeast Asia and increasingly popular in Asian-American gardens — with longer, smoother, pale-white fruits that are notably less bitter than the standard green bitter melon, making them more accessible to those new to this important Asian vegetable.

directions: Direct sow White Bitter Melon seeds in warm soil in full sun after last frost; provide a sturdy trellis or fence and train vines upward for maximum production; water consistently at the base and fertilize every 3–4 weeks with a balanced vegetable fertilizer; harvest fruits when they are pale cream to white and firm, before they begin to yellow. White Bitter Melon is the pale-fruited form of the familiar bitter melon vine (Momordica charantia), grown throughout Southeast Asia, the Indian subcontinent, and diaspora communities worldwide for its distinctive culinary role in curries, stir-fries, and stuffed preparations. The white-skinned variety differs from the standard warty green variety in bearing longer, smoother, paler-green to creamy-white fruits that are generally considered less intensely bitter — making it a better entry point for cooks unfamiliar with the characteristic bitterness, while retaining the distinctive gourd flavor that defines dishes like Kerala's Bitter Melon Thoran and Chinese stir-fried bitter melon with eggs. In zone 9–12, the vine grows vigorously and produces heavily from midsummer through fall; in zone 9, it may return from the root if the crown is protected from frost. Like the standard green bitter melon, the unripe fruits are the primary culinary use; ripe, yellow fruits split to reveal bright-red seed arils that are sweet and edible but the pulp becomes very bitter.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) — Momordica charantia varieties
Taiwan Seed Improvement and Propagation Station — bitter gourd types
University of Florida IFAS Extension — bitter melon production

subcategory: Pacific island sticky-seeded shade coastal tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in zone 10–12; Bird Catcher Tree (Para Para) is a large-leaved tropical tree native to Pacific islands from New Zealand to Hawaii, Australia, Southeast Asia, and East Africa — famous for its extremely sticky, narrow, club-shaped fruits that trap small birds, insects, and lizards, sometimes fatally — and grown ornamentally for its bold, tropical, large-leaved foliage in shaded or semi-shaded positions.

directions: Plant Pisonia umbellifera in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; young trees prefer some protection from the hottest afternoon sun; water regularly during establishment; mature trees are fairly drought-tolerant; the sticky fruits should be considered when siting — place away from areas frequented by small birds and do not plant near bird feeders. Bird Catcher Tree is one of the more ecologically unusual trees of Pacific island forests — its narrow, club-shaped fruits are covered in an extremely sticky, clear resin that adheres to anything that brushes against it, and small birds investigating the fruit clusters can become fatally entangled. This macabre adaptation has made it a subject of scientific and popular fascination: in some Pacific island seabird colonies (particularly on Heron Island, Australia), large numbers of migratory petrels and shearwaters are trapped each breeding season. Despite its sinister seed-dispersal strategy, the Bird Catcher Tree is a handsome ornamental with very large, glossy, paddle-shaped leaves creating a bold tropical texture in the shade garden; it performs well as a specimen or screen in zone 10–12. The variegated form ('Variegata') with green and cream-white blotched leaves is particularly attractive and widely grown as an ornamental across tropical and subtropical regions.
sources: New Zealand Plant Conservation Network — Pisonia umbellifera
Australian Government — Heron Island Bird Catcher Tree research

subcategory: Florida Keys endemic coastal native shrub

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained to dry, rocky, alkaline, sandy coastal soil in zone 10–12; Joewood is a rare, slow-growing, evergreen shrub or small tree native exclusively to the Florida Keys and southernmost Miami-Dade County coastal hammocks — bearing fragrant, small, white, star-shaped flowers in clusters and distinctive, hard-wooded branches — an endemic Florida native of significant conservation concern that provides critical habitat value in coastal hammock ecosystems.

directions: Plant Jacquinia keyensis in rocky, alkaline, well-drained to dry, sandy coastal soil in full sun to partial shade; it is highly adapted to salt spray, thin rocky substrate, and periodic drought, making it an excellent choice for coastal restoration plantings and coastal hammock gardens in the Florida Keys and extreme South Florida; water sparingly once established. Joewood is one of the rarest and most regionally restricted native shrubs in the United States — found naturally only in the Florida Keys and the southern tip of Miami-Dade County, where it grows in coastal hammock communities on porous, alkaline, marl and coral rock soils just above the tidal zone. The species is classified as Threatened in Florida due to habitat loss from development, sea-level rise, and invasive plant competition. Small, leathery, spine-tipped leaves, fragrant white star-flowers produced repeatedly through warm months, and distinctive hard, contorted wood give Joewood a distinct, sculptural appearance suited to both naturalistic coastal plantings and cultivated low-maintenance coastal gardens. It provides nectar for native bees and butterflies and fruit for migratory birds. A slow-growing plant that rewards patience with exceptional performance in otherwise challenging coastal conditions. Sourcing from Florida-native nurseries propagated from local ecotypes is strongly recommended to support regional genetic conservation.
sources: Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services — Jacquinia keyensis Threatened species
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Florida Keys native plants
Florida Native Plant Society — Jacquinia keyensis

subcategory: Bolivian yellow tuberous begonia parent species

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil or container mix in zone 8–10; Yellow Begonia (Pearce's Begonia) is a compact, tuberous begonia species native to the Bolivian Andes — one of the primary parent species of the entire modern tuberous hybrid begonia group — bearing abundant bright-yellow, flat-faced single flowers above distinctive dark-green leaves with silver spotting, and far more heat-tolerant than modern large-flowered tuberous hybrid begonias.

directions: Plant Begonia pearcei tubers in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; water consistently but ensure excellent drainage; fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer; allow foliage to die back in fall and store tubers dry and cool in winter in zone 8–9; repot each spring. Yellow Begonia holds a special place in horticultural history as one of the five wild Bolivian and Peruvian Andean begonias that, when hybridized in Belgium in the 1860s–1870s, produced the entire line of large-flowered tuberous hybrid begonias that became one of the most popular bedding plants in history. Begonia pearcei contributed the yellow flower color — absent from other Andean begonia species — making possible the spectacular range of yellow, orange, and bicolored modern tuberous begonias. The species itself is a charming garden plant: compact, with attractive dark-green leaves marked with silver spots, and producing masses of bright, clear-yellow single flowers in partial shade throughout summer. Unlike the large-flowered modern hybrids which struggle in heat, the species form is more tolerant of warm temperatures. In the garden, it excels in containers, shaded window boxes, and as a groundcover in partially shaded borders. Tubers increase steadily by division over years.
sources: Thompson, E. A., 'The Begonia Growers' Handbook' (Grower Books, 1966)
American Begonia Society — species begonias of the Andes

subcategory: Spineless edible prickly pear cactus cultivar

planting.note: Root a dried, calloused pad cutting in dry, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun in zone 8–11; Spineless Prickly Pear is a cultivated, spineless form of Opuntia ficus-indica — selected and grown without the stinging glochid hairs (and with reduced or absent spines) for easier harvesting and handling of both the young pads (nopales) and ripe fruits (tunas/prickly pears) — widely grown for food production across Mexico, the Mediterranean, South Africa, and increasingly in Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Root a calloused Opuntia pad cutting in dry, coarse, sandy soil in full sun; allow the cut end to dry for 1–2 weeks before planting to prevent rot; plant only 2–3 inches deep; water sparingly until established (3–4 months), then infrequently; harvest young pads in spring and ripe fruits in late summer/fall. Spineless Prickly Pear cultivars lack the dangerous glochid clusters that make wild and spiny Opuntia so irritating to work with, making the harvest of both young pads and ripe fruits safe and practical with only minimal care (lightweight gloves recommended). Young pads (nopales), harvested in spring when 4–6 inches long, are a major vegetable in Mexican cuisine — grilled, diced into salsa, cooked into soups, or scrambled with eggs; they have a mild, slightly mucilaginous, tart-green flavor. The ripe fruits (tunas) in late summer and fall range from yellow to red to deep purple depending on variety; the sweet, melon-like flesh makes excellent juice, sorbet, jam, and candy. The spineless form was famously promoted by Luther Burbank in the early 1900s as a drought-resistant livestock fodder crop for arid lands — though that particular venture was not entirely successful commercially, his selections remain the basis of modern spineless cultivar lines. An outstanding plant for Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens where heat, drought, and alkaline soils challenge most edibles.
sources: USDA ARS — Opuntia ficus-indica spineless cultivar research
University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — nopales production
Luther Burbank Home and Gardens historical archives

subcategory: Premium Moroccan fresh-eating date palm cultivar

planting.note: Plant tissue-culture offshoots in spring in full sun in deep, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in zone 9–11; Medjool Date Palm is the most prized of all date palm cultivars — originally from the royal palm gardens of Morocco and the Middle East — bearing exceptionally large (1.5–3 inch), soft, caramel-sweet, fresh-eating dates with rich, complex flavor considered superior to all other date varieties, and producing a strikingly ornamental, stately palm with gracefully arching, blue-green pinnate fronds.

directions: Plant Medjool Date Palm offshoots (tissue-culture plants or basal offshoots from certified, disease-free sources) in deep, well-drained soil in full sun; requires hot, dry summers (ideally above 95°F) for fruit development and very low humidity during the fruit ripening period — high humidity causes fungal problems with the developing fruit clusters; irrigate deeply but infrequently, allowing soil to dry between waterings. Medjool dates are among the most commercially valuable date palm products in the world — large, soft, caramel-brown dates with rich, complex sweetness described as a combination of honey, caramel, and dried fruit; they are the 'fresh date' sold in upscale markets and gourmet stores. Originally from the royal date gardens of Morocco and the Middle East, Medjool was brought to the United States in 1927 by Walter Swingle of the USDA, who rescued eleven offshoots from a diseased date garden in Morocco; this small genetic founder population gave rise to the entire US commercial Medjool industry centered in the Coachella Valley of California and the Yuma area of Arizona. For fruiting in Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens, Medjool requires the dry, intensely hot summers of desert climates; in humid coastal areas like Houston, fruiting is unreliable but the palm grows magnificently as an ornamental. Must be hand-pollinated by transferring pollen from male palm flowers to female date clusters to ensure fruit set in home gardens.
sources: University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Medjool date palm cultivation
UC Cooperative Extension Riverside County — date palm production
USDA GRIN — Phoenix dactylifera Medjool history
